Description: User can authenticate and start a session
How It Works: Laravel session auth — always available.
Notes: All accounts get this without exception.

Description: User can end their own session
How It Works: Laravel session auth — always available.
Notes: Always granted.

Description: User can see their own profile page
How It Works: No permission check needed. Tied to authenticated session.
Notes: Identity operation — not permission-gated.

Description: User can update their own name / email / CNIC / phone / address
How It Works: No permission check needed.
Notes: Identity operation — not permission-gated.

Description: User can update their own password
How It Works: No permission check needed.
Notes: Identity operation — not permission-gated.

Description: User can permanently delete their own account
How It Works: No permission check needed.
Notes: Identity operation — not permission-gated.

Description: User can access the main /dashboard page
How It Works: Add ->middleware('permission:dashboard.view') to the route.
Notes: Every account lands here after login.

Description: Reveals total users count / all devices count / system-wide online count on the dashboard
How It Works: DashboardController checks can('dashboard.view_system_stats') before building the system stats array. @can in Blade hides the 4-card admin strip.
Notes: Hidden from regular users by default. Grant only to trusted operators.

Description: User can see the device list filtered to devices assigned to them
How It Works: DeviceManagementController uses scopeForUser() when user lacks devices.view_any.
Notes: Every account gets this. Without it the device list would be empty and useless.

Description: User can see every device in the system regardless of owner
How It Works: DeviceManagementController drops the user_id filter. DevicePolicy::view() allows access.
Notes: Grants admin-level device visibility. Required for operators managing the full fleet.

Description: User can fill in the Add Device form and register any new device type
How It Works: Route gets ->middleware('permission:devices.create'). Controller checks permission before processing.
Notes: Do not grant to end-consumers by default. Grant to field engineers or operators.

Description: User can edit name / MQTT topic / settings of devices they own
How It Works: DeviceManagementController checks can('devices.edit_own') && device->user_id === user->id.
Notes: Useful for prosumer accounts that self-manage their hardware.

Description: User can edit any device regardless of owner
How It Works: DeviceManagementController checks can('devices.edit_any') as override.
Notes: Implies edit_own. Typically operator/admin level.

Description: User can delete devices they own
How It Works: DevicePolicy::delete() checks delete_own + ownership. Controller enforces it.
Notes: Destructive — grant with care.

Description: User can delete any device regardless of owner
How It Works: DevicePolicy::delete() checks delete_any as override.
Notes: Most destructive device permission. Typically only Super Admin level.

Description: User can set/change which user a device belongs to (the Assign To User dropdown in create/edit)
How It Works: DeviceManagementController shows the user dropdown only when can('devices.assign_owner') is true. Assignment logic is also gated.
Notes: Required for anyone who needs to provision devices to specific users.

Description: Master switch — user is allowed to interact with meters at all. Without this permission the device dashboard for any meter type shows a 'not permitted' placeholder instead of the live view.
How It Works: DeviceDashboardController::show() checks can('meter.access') before calling showMeter(). If absent it returns the placeholder view with a 'no access' reason — identical to how inactive devices are handled today.
Notes: This is the top-level gate for the entire meter system. All meter.* section permissions below are meaningless without this. Assign this first, then grant individual sections.

Description: User can add/register their own meter-type devices. This is narrower than devices.create — it only permits the meter type and always assigns ownership to self (no Assign To User dropdown, no other device types).
How It Works: DeviceManagementController::store() checks: if user has devices.create → full create. If user has meter.self_provision → allow only if type==='meter' and force user_id to auth()->id(). Blade shows a stripped-down form with type locked to Meter.
Notes: Use this when you want a user to onboard their own physical meter without giving them full device-management access. Super Admin still assigns meters directly when self-provisioning is not granted.

Description: User can see meters assigned to them by Super Admin. This is already covered by devices.view_own — listed here for clarity: no extra permission needed. Super Admin creates the device and sets user_id to the target user.
How It Works: No additional permission required. Devices assigned by Super Admin automatically appear in the user's device list.
Notes: Included here for documentation clarity only. No new permission slug needed.

Description: User can change ONLY the display name of their own meter device. All other fields — MQTT topic, availability topic, device type, active/inactive toggle, and assigned user — are locked. Only Super Admin (via devices.edit_own / devices.edit_any) can change those fields.
How It Works: DeviceManagementController::edit() passes $nameOnly = $user->can('meter.rename') && !$user->can('devices.edit_own') && !$user->can('devices.edit_any') to the view. Blade renders all fields as read-only/hidden except name when $nameOnly is true. DeviceManagementController::update() enforces this server-side: when nameOnly mode, the validated array is stripped down to ['name' => $request->name] only — all other inputs are ignored regardless of what was POSTed.
Notes: This is the default editing capability for regular meter users. It lets them personalise their meter label (e.g. 'Kitchen Meter') without touching the hardware config. Super Admin retains exclusive control over MQTT topics, device type, and active state.

Description: User can see the 8 live KPI cards at the top of the meter dashboard: Voltage, Current, Power, Computed Energy, PZEM Energy, Frequency, Power Factor, Last Reading timestamp. These are always the most recent snapshot — independent of any time range.
How It Works: DeviceDashboardController::showMeter() passes $canViewLiveData = $user->can('meter.live_data') to the view. Blade wraps the .kpi-grid div in @if($canViewLiveData). The JS updateKPIs() and the status poll (API_STATUS) are conditionally skipped if the section is not rendered.
Notes: Without this, the user still sees the device header (name, health pill, availability pill) and status banners — they just can't see the numeric readings. This is intentional: health state is informational, not a data privilege.

Description: User can see the 5 Chart.js charts: Voltage+Current (dual axis), Active Power, Energy Comparison (Computed vs PZEM), Grid Frequency, Power Factor bars. Chart data is sampled up to 500 points over the selected time range.
How It Works: DeviceDashboardController::showMeter() passes $canViewCharts. Blade wraps the .section-wrap.charts-grid in @if($canViewCharts). JS skips initializing Chart.js and calling API_CHART if the canvas elements are absent. DeviceReadingController::chart() adds abort_unless(can('meter.charts')) check.
Notes: Chart.js library script tag is also conditionally pushed in @push('styles') only when $canViewCharts is true — no unnecessary CDN load.

Description: User can see the paginated readings table with timestamp, voltage, current, power, computed energy, PZEM energy, frequency, PF columns. Navigable by page (100 rows/page). Affected by the time range selector.
How It Works: DeviceDashboardController::showMeter() passes $canViewHistory. Blade wraps the readings .section-wrap and .pagination-bar in @if($canViewHistory). JS skips table fetch and pagination setup if the table element is absent. DeviceReadingController::index() adds abort_unless(can('meter.history')) check.
Notes: The range selector bar (1H / 6H / 24H / Today / 7D / 30D / All + custom picker) is shown only if EITHER meter.charts OR meter.history is granted — both sections share it.

Description: Controls the time window for charts and the historical table. This is NOT a separate permission — it is shown automatically when at least one of meter.charts or meter.history is granted.
How It Works: $canShowRangeBar = $canViewCharts || $canViewHistory passed to view. Blade wraps the .controls-bar and .custom-range-card in @if($canShowRangeBar).
Notes: No new permission slug needed. The range bar has no value if neither section it controls is visible.

Description: User gets the full-resolution operator dashboard (raw minute-level readings, all 8 KPI cards, monthly/daily report panels). WITHOUT it a meter.access user gets the SIMPLIFIED consumer dashboard: 4 KPI tiles (Voltage, Power, Monthly Units, Daily Units) plus a click-to-expand Usage History section served as hour/day aggregate buckets (units + avg V/W).
How It Works: DeviceDashboardController routes on User::hasFullMeterDashboard() (meter.full_dashboard OR meter.charts — the charts opt-in only exists on the full view) to meter.blade.php vs meter-simple.blade.php. DeviceReadingController::index() additionally requires the same predicate, so raw rows cannot be fetched by hand-crafted requests; aggregate() serves the simplified history under meter.access + meter.history from the meter_hourly_consumption / meter_daily_consumption rollups.
Notes: Product decision 2026-07-14. In the prosumer bundle; deliberately NOT in consumer. Hour rows are pruned at 180 days (meters:prune-hourly-consumption); older windows degrade to day buckets automatically.

Description: User can access the paginated /users page with search and filter
How It Works: Route uses ->middleware('permission:users.view_list'). Controller abort_unless check.
Notes: Entry-level user management permission.

Description: User can open an individual user's detail page showing their info and devices
How It Works: abort_unless(can('users.view_profile')) at top of show().
Notes: Depends on users.view_list to navigate to the page.

Description: User can access the Add User form and create new user accounts
How It Works: Route uses ->middleware('permission:users.create'). New accounts automatically get built-in permissions on creation.
Notes: New accounts still have only built-in permissions — separate grant required after creation.

Description: User can edit another user's name / email / CNIC / phone / address
How It Works: abort_unless(can('users.edit')) in edit() and update().
Notes: Does NOT include changing permissions — that requires users.manage_permissions.

Description: User can permanently delete other users and their data
How It Works: Route kept under role:super_admin middleware. Only Super Admin can delete accounts.
Notes: Very destructive. Reserved for Super Admin only.

Description: User can open the per-user permission management screen and toggle each permission on or off
How It Works: New PermissionController::show() and update(). Route under role:super_admin. Accessible via 'Manage Permissions' link on users/show.blade.php.
Notes: The most powerful non-Super-Admin permission. Granting it is effectively delegating Super Admin authority.

Description: Token can call GET /api/devices and GET /api/devices/{id} and GET /api/devices/{id}/status
How It Works: Api/DeviceController checks hasPermissionTo('api.devices.read' guard:'sanctum'). Device list still filtered by ownership unless devices.view_any is also granted.
Notes: Built-in for API consumers. Requires Sanctum token.

Description: Token can call the readings and chart data endpoints
How It Works: DeviceReadingController checks hasPermissionTo('api.readings.read' guard:'sanctum').
Notes: Built-in. Required for any dashboard or mobile app consuming live data.

Description: Token can call POST /api/devices and DELETE /api/devices/{id}
How It Works: DevicePolicy::create() and delete() check api.devices.write. Triggered by $this->authorize() calls already in Api/DeviceController.
Notes: Grant only to provisioning services or trusted automation. Destructive on DELETE.
